title: Western Wildfire Support Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Western Wildfire Support Act of 2025 helps the government better handle wildfires from start to finish. The bill creates special emergency teams to help areas right after fires happen and sets up money to fix damaged land and buildings over time. It also speeds up getting new technology like sensors and cameras to spot fires early, gives money to Native American tribes for firefighting equipment, and funds research on using drones to fight fires. The law requires the government to use the best science when planning how to manage fires and makes sure states get paid back when military training accidentally causes fires.
last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources. Ordered to be reported with an amendment in the nature of a substitute favorably.
subjects: Social work, volunteer service, charitable organizations
Wilderness and natural areas, wildlife refuges, wild rivers, habitats

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title; Table of Contents
sections.text: This law can be called the Western Wildfire Support Act of 2025. The law is organized into different sections that cover three main areas. Title 1 deals with preparation for wildfires, including making firefighting accounts more transparent, getting money back when military training causes fires, planning how to manage fires, and studying how to use local firefighters better. Title 2 covers equipment and support for detecting and fighting wildfires, including detection equipment, special fire trucks, research on unmanned aircraft, and studies on drones and new technologies. Title 3 focuses on help after fires are over, including online guides for disaster help, managing burned areas, creating a special account for long-term recovery, and prizes for reducing invasive plants after fires.

sections.title: Section 2  Definitions
sections.text: This section explains what certain terms mean in this law. Congressional committees means specific committees in the Senate and House of Representatives that deal with natural resources and money for government programs. Federal land includes public lands managed by different government agencies, national parks, wildlife refuges, land held in trust for Native American tribes, and national forests. A fireshed is an area of forest where a fire could threaten homes, communities, or important buildings and infrastructure. The National Forest System means forests managed by the government, but does not include grasslands or forests east of the one hundredth meridian (a line running through the middle of the United States). A resource management plan is a document that guides how land should be managed. Secretaries means the Secretary of the Interior and the Secretary of Agriculture, who are the government officials in charge of different types of federal land. Secretary concerned means whichever Secretary is responsible for the specific piece of land being discussed.

sections.title: Section 101  Firefighting Account Transparency
sections.text: This section changes the rules for how the government reports on money spent fighting wildfires. The government must now provide annual reports about how much money was spent from wildfire management accounts each year, including any extra emergency funding used. The reports must include detailed information about catastrophic wildfires, which are defined as fires that require federal firefighting help and either burn at least one hundred thousand acres or burn at least fifty thousand acres with half or more burned severely. These catastrophic fires must also either cost fifty million dollars or more to fight, destroy at least one home, or directly cause at least one death. The reports must break down costs by different categories including ground operations, aircraft operations, personnel, support costs, administrative costs, and other relevant factors.

sections.title: Section 102  Reimbursement for Wildfires Caused by Military Training
sections.text: The Department of Defense must make agreements with state agencies to help each other fight fires. When military training or other planned military activities cause a fire, the Department of Defense must pay back the state agency for the cost of fighting that fire. The state agency must provide a detailed list of services and costs when asking for money back. Only services directly related to fighting the specific fire can be reimbursed. The Department of Defense will use money from its operation and maintenance budget to make these payments. Any agreements that already exist when this law takes effect will count as agreements under this new law if they meet the requirements.

sections.title: Section 103  Strategic Wildland Fire Management Planning
sections.text: By September thirtieth, 2026, the government officials in charge of federal land must review and update fire management policies for each fireshed on federal land using the best available science and planning tools. These policies must be updated regularly, including within one year after a wildfire happens in the area and at least once every ten years. The policies must identify wildfire and smoke risks to first responders, communities, important infrastructure, and valuable resources. They must be consistent with other land management plans and work with states to identify places where fires could be controlled and areas where firefighters might face extra danger due to steep slopes, dangerous fuels, or other hazards. The policies must also describe what weather conditions would create severe fire conditions and include other planning provisions for wildfire response. When developing or updating land management plans, the government should include an employee who helped develop fire management policies for that area.

sections.title: Section 104  Study and Report on Integrating Local Firefighters into Wildfire Response
sections.text: Within one year of this law taking effect, the Secretary of Homeland Security, working with the U.S. Fire Administration and the National Wildfire Coordinating Group, must study gaps in training for structural firefighters in areas with high wildfire risk and submit a report to congressional committees. The report must include a summary of how federal wildfire fighters currently work with state, local, or tribal firefighters. It must analyze the differences in how these firefighters respond to fires that threaten a single building versus fires that threaten an entire community. The report must identify existing training programs available through the National Fire Academy for firefighters dealing with community-threatening wildfires, or gaps in such training. It must also estimate the cost and create a spending plan to address any gaps in training programs.

sections.title: Section 201  Wildfire Detection Equipment
sections.text: The government officials in charge of federal land must work to speed up the placement of wildfire detection equipment like sensors and cameras in areas at risk of wildfire. They must expand the use of satellite data to improve wildfire detection and response, and speed up any permits needed for installing, maintaining, or removing detection equipment. They must use unmanned aerial vehicles to assess wildfires in their early stages to determine the right response actions. They must review permitting and purchasing requirements for detection equipment to account for modern and innovative technology. Each year, they must provide a forum for companies developing and testing new wildfire technology to meet with wildfire managers.

sections.title: Section 202  Slip-on Tanker Units
sections.text: This section expands a program that provides financial help to buy firefighting slip-on tanker units (portable water tanks that can be mounted on trucks) to include Native American tribes, not just local governments. The Secretary of the Interior must submit annual reports to congressional committees about this program, including how many units were purchased with government help by state, how many requests were received, and any barriers that prevent local governments and tribes from participating. The first report is due by October first, 2026, and the reporting requirement ends on October first, 2028. The Secretaries must work with people who receive this financial help and the U.S. Fire Administration to create guidance for using these units in wildfire response, include them in resource tracking systems when possible, and collaborate on necessary training for operators.

sections.title: Section 203  Research and Development of Unmanned Aircraft System Fire Applications
sections.text: An unmanned aircraft system means an unmanned aircraft and all the parts needed to operate it safely, including communication links and control components. The Secretaries must work through the Joint Fire Science Program with universities and other research institutions to research and develop how unmanned aircraft systems can be used in wildfire response. They may work with the Federal Aviation Administration to test these systems at unmanned aircraft test ranges according to federal law. Congress authorizes whatever money is necessary for the Secretaries to carry out this section.

sections.title: Section 204  Study on Drone Incursions on Wildfire Suppression
sections.text: This section defines terms used in the study. Administrator means the head of the Federal Aviation Administration. A drone is an unmanned aircraft owned by a private person or company. A drone incursion happens when someone flies a drone in airspace where the Federal Aviation Administration has banned flights because of a wildfire. The Federal Aviation Administration Administrator, working with the Interior and Agriculture Secretaries, must study how drone incursions affect wildfire suppression on federal land. The study must determine for the past five years how many times drone incursions interfered with fighting wildfires and estimate the effects on suppression time, delays in aerial firefighting units, and federal costs. The study must also evaluate different ways to prevent drone incursions, including counter-drone radio towers, using reasonable force to disable drones, seizing drones with nets, and educating people about how drone incursions affect wildfire fighting. Within eighteen months of this law taking effect, the Administrator must submit a report with the study findings and recommendations to several congressional committees.

sections.title: Section 205  Study on Modernizing Wildfire Response Technologies
sections.text: The Secretaries must study improvements needed for radio communications during wildfire operations, including assessing the quality and reliability of existing radio infrastructure, impacts when radio systems fail, comparing options to improve ground communications, and analyzing costs and timelines to install the best option. They must also study real-time situational awareness tools for firefighters, including standards to ensure different agencies can work together, requirements for additional remote sensing and mapping, and cost comparisons between commercial and internally developed systems. The study must cover wildfire predictive modeling, including analyzing data needed to reduce errors in existing models, computing needs for faster or more accurate modeling, the feasibility of using artificial intelligence, and existing models that could help establish safe conditions for prescribed fires. Within one year of this law taking effect, the Secretaries must submit the study results to congressional committees and make them available to the public.

sections.title: Section 301  Funding for Online Guides for Post-Disaster Assistance
sections.text: This section changes existing disaster relief law to add post-disaster assistance to the list of services that government agencies can provide to each other. It allows the Federal Emergency Management Agency Administrator to make agreements with state agencies to provide money or technical help to create, update, or operate websites that give information about recovery funding and resources to communities or individuals affected by major disasters or emergencies. These websites must be managed by the state agency and suitable for residents of that state. The websites can provide lists of federal, state, and local sources of recovery funding for communities and individuals, and technical guides explaining costs and benefits of different ways to reduce disaster impacts and prepare for related hazards like flooding after wildfires. State agencies receiving this help must work with various federal departments and agencies in creating and operating these websites. The websites must be updated at least once every one hundred eighty days.

sections.title: Section 302  Post-Fire Management and Recovery
sections.text: Within one hundred eighty days of this law taking effect, the government official in charge of the affected federal land must create one or more permanent Burned Area Emergency Response Teams to coordinate immediate post-wildfire emergency work and erosion planning. These teams must survey landscapes affected by wildfires to determine burn severity, identify and remove dead or dying trees that might pose immediate danger to people, communities, or critical infrastructure, stabilize or remove hazardous materials from wildfires that might immediately endanger people, communities, or the environment, conduct emergency landscape stabilization and erosion prevention work with public safety as the priority, prevent the spread of invasive species, help inform the public about wildfire hazards, work with activities under section 303, and conduct other work as determined necessary. To the maximum extent possible, these teams should coordinate and respond to immediate post-wildfire emergency stabilization and erosion planning needs for no more than one year after a wildfire is contained. The government official in charge can use money made available by existing infrastructure law to carry out this section.

sections.title: Section 303  Long-Term Burned Area Rehabilitation Account
sections.text: This section creates a new account in the U.S. Treasury called the Long-Term Burned Area Rehabilitation account for the Department of Agriculture. Congress authorizes up to one hundred million dollars each year starting in fiscal year 2025 for this account. Starting in fiscal year 2026, the Secretary of Agriculture must request money for this account through the President's budget. The Secretary must use money from this account for rehabilitation projects at sites affected by wildfires or post-wildfire flooding, mainly on federal land but potentially including some non-federal land. Projects can restore ecosystem functions by identifying areas where natural regrowth is unlikely, revegetating and replanting forests including site preparation and salvage harvesting, restoring watersheds, controlling and removing invasive species, and restoring wildlife habitat. Projects can also repair or replace infrastructure or facilities needed for land management activities. Projects must begin after the wildfire is contained and be completed within five years of containment. The Secretary can make agreements with non-federal entities to carry out these activities, with the non-federal share of costs being no more than twenty percent and including in-kind contributions. The Secretary must prioritize projects nationwide based on downstream effects on water resources. Starting in fiscal year 2027, the Secretary must submit annual reports to Congress on burned area recovery work using money from this new account and other existing funding sources.

sections.title: Section 304  Prize for Wildfire-Related Invasive Species Reduction
sections.text: This section amends existing law to create a new prize competition called the Theodore Roosevelt Genius Prize for the management of wildfire-related invasive species. The Secretary must establish this prize competition within one hundred eighty days of this law taking effect to encourage technological innovation for managing invasive species related to wildfires and to award one or more prizes annually. The section creates the Management of Wildfire-Related Invasive Species Technology Advisory Board with at least nine members appointed by the Secretary who have expertise in invasive species, biology, technology development, engineering, economics, business development and management, wildfire, and other necessary disciplines. The Board must select topics, issue problem statements, advise the Secretary about technological innovation opportunities, and advise winners about opportunities to pilot and implement winning technologies with various partners. The Board must consult widely with federal and non-federal stakeholders including various agencies and organizations with jurisdiction over invasive species and wildfire management. The Secretary, working through the Executive Director of the National Invasive Species Council, will administer the prize competition. The Secretary will appoint at least three judges to select annual winners, but the Secretary can determine that no entry merits an award in any fiscal year. Within sixty days of awarding a prize, the Secretary must submit a report to congressional committees describing the Board's activities, the winners, and the judges' reasoning for their selection. The Board and all authority under this section end on December thirty-first, 2028.
